stewart_read: Kevin is probing for local legitimacy — 'are you around here?' is a soft version of 'who are you and why should I trust you?' The rep's response ('Yeah. We have you here in Illinois. Correct?') deflects the question back to address verification rather than anchoring local presence. The codex principle on specificity reducing suspicion applies here: a brief, confident local anchor ('We cover the Des Plaines area — we've done installs nearby') would have converted this probe into a trust moment.
alternative_hypothesis: The rep may have read this as a logistical question about in-person visits and answered accordingly — not a deliberate deflection.
coaching_implication: Show Brandon this exchange. Ask: what was Kevin actually asking? Then work the local-legitimacy anchor as a 15-second move that costs nothing and pays in trust.

quote: Did did I talk to you earlier today? Or did my
stewart_read: Kevin's question about whether he spoke to someone earlier is an excessive-contact signal — he or someone in his household may have already fielded an Ion call today. The rep's response ('No. I don't I don't believe we had spoken today') is honest but does not address the underlying concern (am I being over-contacted?). The rep then pivots immediately to bill collection without acknowledging the friction. This is relevant handoff context: if Kevin has already been contacted once today, the closer should know.
alternative_hypothesis: Kevin may simply be confused about which company called — not necessarily an excessive-contact complaint. The rep's honest denial is the correct factual response.
coaching_implication: Flag for handoff brief discipline: the closer should know Kevin may have had a prior Ion touch today. Also coach Brandon to briefly acknowledge the confusion ('Sounds like you've been getting a few calls — I'll make this quick') rather than pivoting past it.

quote: I do. It's my home. And then they're gonna put it where I want it and they ain't gonna put nothing.
stewart_read: The household argument surfaces the daughter's active resistance in real time — she is audibly skeptical and Kevin is defending his interest in solar against her. The rep does not address this in the moment: no acknowledgment of the tension, no reframe of the appointment as the venue where her questions get answered. The rep waits for the argument to subside and then asks about the Sunrun quote at 11:11 — a missed opportunity to address the daughter's concerns when they were live. The codex protocol calls for including the co-decision-maker, not waiting for the argument to pass.
alternative_hypothesis: The rep may have deliberately stayed out of the household dynamic to avoid taking sides — a reasonable instinct. But staying out is different from not acknowledging it at all.
coaching_implication: Show Brandon the 06:50 moment and the 12:55 moment together. The daughter's resistance was visible and audible — the right move was to surface it directly: 'It sounds like your daughter has some questions too — that's exactly what the appointment is for. Let's make sure she's there.' Coach the reframe, not just the inclusion ask.

quote: Yeah. And I was thinking pretty serious about getting some of it.
stewart_read: This is the call's strongest buy signal — unprompted, unambiguous, and delivered after Kevin has already navigated the household argument and the bill-photo friction. Kevin is telling the rep he wants this. The rep's response is to ask about the Sunrun quote ('What did Sunrun say?') — a competitive-intel move that is procedurally reasonable but misses the emotional peak entirely. The correct move is to reflect the signal back and anchor it before pivoting to competitive context.
alternative_hypothesis: The Sunrun question is a legitimate competitive-intel gather that the rep needed before booking — not a pure miss.
coaching_implication: Coach Brandon to hear buy signals and reflect them before pivoting. A suggested rephrase: 'That's exactly where we want to be — let's make sure the design we build for you backs that up.' Then go to Sunrun. The signal should be named and anchored, not walked past.

quote: well, once I get the energy bill, we will get started on a design here for your home. And, you know, we're pretty competitive. So whatever Sunrun quoted you, we'll be more than happy to compare with them and and hopefully get you a more cheaper, affordable energy bill.
stewart_read: The rep has the bill in hand (photo received at 12:39) and Kevin has just disclosed his bill range ($29-$88). The rep's response is a generic competitive-comparison pitch — 'we're pretty competitive' — rather than an emotional flip on the bill pain. The codex is explicit: bill capture alone is not sufficient; the bill must be flipped into emotional alignment-against-the-utility. Kevin's $88 summer bill is the lever. The rep drops it.
alternative_hypothesis: The rep may have judged that Kevin's bill is relatively low and that a bill-flip would not land with the same force as on a $200+ bill. This is a reasonable read but not grounded in anything the rep said.
coaching_implication: This is the universal floor coaching gap per the codex. Show Brandon the capture-and-drop pattern. The suggested move: name the bill, name the utility, and redirect the frustration — 'ComEd is going to keep charging you that $88 every summer, and it goes up. The design we build will show you what you could be paying instead.' No fabricated numbers needed — the pain is in the pattern, not the amount.

quote: Yeah. Great. We would love to also meet with your daughter. I know, she might be a little hesitant to meet with us.
stewart_read: The rep does eventually attempt daughter inclusion — credit for that. But 'she might be a little hesitant' validates her resistance as a given rather than reframing the appointment as the venue where her questions get resolved. The codex template is: 'Let's make sure [name] is on the appointment with us. What time works for both of you?' — no concession to hesitance, just inclusion as the natural next step. The rep's framing gives the daughter a reason to opt out.
alternative_hypothesis: The rep may have been trying to lower Kevin's anxiety about getting his daughter to agree — acknowledging her hesitance as a way of normalizing it. But the effect is the opposite: it signals to Kevin that her resistance is expected and legitimate.
coaching_implication: Replace 'she might be a little hesitant' with a reframe that positions the appointment as the answer to her questions. The daughter's skepticism is an asset if the closer can address it directly — but only if she's in the room.

quote: Okay. Great. I have you on our calendar for Tuesday. That's the twenty first at 09:30AM.
stewart_read: The appointment is booked but the button-up is incomplete. Missing: one-hour block stated, no-driving/at-computer anchor, 'is that fair' handshake, and text-followup promise. All four are required per the codex. The no-driving/computer anchor is particularly relevant here — Kevin is older, navigating a household with an active daughter, and has already shown tech friction during bill collection. Without the anchor, no-show risk is elevated.
coaching_implication: Run Brandon through the button-up checklist against this call. Each missing element has a specific no-show risk attached. The 'is that fair' handshake is the verbal commitment that predicts show rate — it was not obtained here.

quote: So I pull up the home, and I see you've got some some big trees around the house.
stewart_read: The rep surfaces the shading concern from satellite but has no plan for what to do with the answer. When Kevin declines tree removal and explains his roof geometry, the rep responds with 'K. K. But I'm not too sure. Well, let's see what we can do for you.' — an unresolved viability question that is never documented. Shading is not a hard DQ per the codex, but the closer needs to know the roof geometry and the shading context to assess viability on arrival. The rep neither validates Kevin's explanation nor flags it for handoff.
alternative_hypothesis: The rep may have been trying to surface a real concern early rather than booking a potentially unviable lead — a reasonable instinct. But the correct move is to note the uncertainty and route it to the closer, not to leave it unresolved.
coaching_implication: Coach Brandon that shading is not a DQ — it's a closer-tier viability question. The setter's job is to document what Kevin described (flat top section, north-facing slope, one tree) and flag it in the handoff brief. The closer assesses on arrival. Booking with an undocumented viability question is the gap, not booking itself.
